# Execution Plan: Sandboxing for Autonomous Agent Environments (90-Day Roadmap)

**Objective:** Deliver a robust agent sandboxing feature within 90 days, supporting both *enterprise-risk* (container-based isolation with seccomp/network policies) and *high-assurance* (VM-based isolation via Kata/gVisor/Firecracker) modes. The plan spans an MVP, a controlled pilot, and a production rollout, balancing a **central platform approach** (core infrastructure, security guardrails) with **self-service onboarding** for product teams (templates, documentation, admission controls).

<!-- Copilot-Researcher-Visualization -->

![Visualization](data:image/png;base64,)

Each phase is organized around key workstreams: **Platform (infrastructure & runtime)**, **Security & Policy**, **Observability & Compliance**, and **Developer Experience (DevEx)**. These teams work in parallel, with major integration points noted.

***

## Phase 0 (Week 0–2): Design & Alignment

**Key Activities:**

*   **Threat Modeling & Requirements:** Conduct a security workshop to enumerate threats (malicious or unintended code execution, cross-session/cross-tenant data leakage, network-based data exfiltration, persistence of tainted state across runs) and define sandbox requirements. Internal guidance (XPIA Risk Review Playbook) explicitly calls out core requirements: the code must run in a sandbox, the sandbox must not be shared between users/tenants, each newly generated code run must use a new sandbox to prevent taint, and the sandbox must not permit arbitrary network ingress/egress. Classify agent workloads by risk level (enterprise-internal vs. multi-tenant or high-stakes) to determine when to apply container vs. VM isolation. [\[Provision...ox runtime | ADO Work Item (FluidFramework)\]](https://dev.azure.com/fluidframework/235294da-091d-4c29-84fc-cdfc3d90890b/_workitems/edit/70512) [\[Validate s...compliance | ADO Work Item (FluidFramework)\]](https://dev.azure.com/fluidframework/235294da-091d-4c29-84fc-cdfc3d90890b/_workitems/edit/70516)

*   **Architecture Finalization:** Refine the sandbox architecture with stakeholder input. Confirm decisions:
    *   Use **AKS** for most internal workloads; add standalone Firecracker microVM for non-containerizable or on-premises needs.
    *   Use **Kubernetes Pod Sandboxing (Kata Containers)** for high-assurance mode on AKS. The solution architecture comprises four components: the **Azure Linux container host for AKS**, **Microsoft Hyper-V Hypervisor**, the open-source **Cloud-Hypervisor** Virtual Machine Monitor (VMM), and integration with **Kata Container** for the runtime. For GKE environments, use **gVisor**. For non-Kubernetes (on-prem or specialized needs), plan to use Firecracker or QEMU (with KVM) microVMs.
    *   Default to **enterprise-risk mode** (runc + seccomp/AppArmor, no network/host FS) for all agents, with the ability to override to VM-isolation for specific high-risk workloads (via K8s RuntimeClass `kata-vm-isolation`).
    *   Ensure design covers cross-org capability flow (token chain verification, designated executor enforcement) even if not fully utilized until Phase 2 pilot.

*   **Success Metrics Definition:** Set measurable goals for each phase:
    *   Phase 1: All known privilege escalation exploits blocked (validated via red-team scripts); added per-call latency <1ms or <5% CPU overhead; no changes required to agent code beyond adding token retrieval and using proxy.
    *   Phase 2: Zero privilege escalation across delegation chains in tests; successful integration with at least one partner's system using capability tokens.
    *   Phase 3: 100% of targeted agent workloads running in sandboxed environment; no high-severity security incidents in first operational period; <5% performance degradation for typical agent tasks.

*   **Team Formation & Roles:** Assemble a cross-functional tiger team (8–10 people):
    *   **Platform/Infrastructure Lead** – K8s clusters, VM environment, networking
    *   **Security/Compliance Lead** – policies (seccomp, AppArmor, NetworkPolicy), threat modeling, risk sign-offs
    *   **Runtime/Dev Lead** – modifies agent frameworks to use tokens and proxy; integrates in-process policy engine with sandbox
    *   **Observability Engineer** – logging, monitoring, SIEM integration for sandbox events
    *   **Developer Experience Lead** – onboarding materials, templates (Helm charts/Terraform modules), training
    *   **Project Manager** – sprint coordination, stakeholder updates, risk tracking

*   **Environment Prep:** Set up initial dev/test environment:
    *   Create a sandbox *development cluster* (or use an existing non-prod AKS) with necessary features enabled. AKS supports Pod Sandboxing on **Kubernetes version 1.27.0 and higher**. When a pod uses the Kata runtimeClass, the hypervisor spins up a lightweight virtual machine with its own kernel for the workload to operate in.
    *   Implement CI pipelines for building sandbox container images and deploying K8s manifests. Create separate images for the base agent runtime (minimal OS and pre-installed agent dependencies) and for reference monitor proxies.

**Deliverables by end of Phase 0:**

*   **Sandbox Architecture & Threat Model Document** (classification of workloads and target isolation modes, citing internal compliance guidelines such as the XPIA Risk Review Playbook). [\[Validate s...compliance | ADO Work Item (FluidFramework)\]](https://dev.azure.com/fluidframework/235294da-091d-4c29-84fc-cdfc3d90890b/_workitems/edit/70516)
*   **Project Plan & Backlog** (detailed tasks for each workstream for Phases 1–3, with owners).
*   **Test/Dev Cluster** provisioned with required features (e.g., AKS with Kata Container runtime; gVisor on GKE dev cluster; optionally a CI environment with Firecracker for non-K8s testing).
*   **Stakeholder Alignment**: Sign-off from Security and Dev leadership on plan, threat model, and success criteria.

**Exit Criteria (Phase 0):**

*   Stakeholders agree on the sandbox design and success metrics (document approved by Security, Compliance, Platform teams).
*   Development environment ready: repositories created, pipelines running, cluster(s) provisioned.
*   Team trained on the plan, roles defined (platform vs. product team responsibilities).
*   Kickoff meeting held; Phase 1 user stories/tasks in the backlog with owners.

***

## Phase 1 (Week 3–6): MVP – Baseline Sandboxing (Enterprise-Risk Mode)

Focus on **establishing the basic sandbox and ensuring existing agents run with minimal modifications** under constrained conditions.

### Workstream A – Platform/Infrastructure Setup

*   **Kubernetes Baseline:** Implement namespace and node pool for sandboxed agents. For AKS, create a dedicated node pool with Azure Pod Sandboxing enabled (Kata VM isolation) alongside a standard pool for less sensitive agents. Pod Sandboxing on AKS provides VM-based isolation and a separate kernel for each pod. Pod Sandboxing allows resource allocation per pod without sharing with other Kata Containers or namespace containers on the same host.

*   **Sandbox Lifecycle Management:** Leverage the **kubernetes-sigs/agent-sandbox** project, which provides a layered Kubernetes-native architecture for managing isolated, stateful, singleton workloads. It is structured in three tiers: a **Core Layer** managing the `Sandbox` CRD (providing a stable identity and persistent storage for a single Pod), an **Extensions Layer** orchestrating Sandbox resources through templates, claims, and pre-warmed pools to reduce startup latency (`SandboxClaim`, `SandboxWarmPool`), and a **Connectivity & SDK Layer** providing the `sandbox-router` for traffic ingress and Python/Go SDKs for programmatic lifecycle management. Each Sandbox has a stable hostname and network identity. [\[upscend.com\]](https://www.upscend.com/blogs/how-to-build-innovation-sandbox-in-90-days-playbook)

*   **CI/CD Integration:** Develop automation (Terraform/Helm scripts) to deploy the reference monitor service and required K8s objects (RuntimeClass definitions for Kata, NetworkPolicies, ConfigMaps for seccomp/AppArmor profiles, etc.). In non-K8s scenarios, script the stand-up of a Firecracker microVM or Docker container with proper flags.

*   **Reference Monitor ("Tool Gateway") Deployment:** Stand up the initial version of the sidecar/daemon:
    *   **Mode:** For MVP, allow mode with logging. It logs all agent tool usage to build a profile of required capabilities but does not yet enforce capability checks.
    *   **Implementation:** A local HTTP proxy (e.g., Envoy or custom lightweight Python/Go proxy) that intercepts outbound HTTP and certain system calls from the agent.

### Workstream B – Security & Policy

*   **Seccomp and AppArmor Profiles:** Adopt a "baseline" seccomp profile (e.g., Docker's `RuntimeDefault`) and a restrictive AppArmor profile as MVP. Block `clone` with flags that allow new namespaces, block raw socket access. Ensure `no_new_privs` is set on the container runtime to prevent privilege escalation.

*   **Network Policy:** Apply a cluster-wide default **deny egress** policy for agent pods, with exceptions only to the capability gateway and any essential internal services. The sandbox denies all outbound network access by default; any attempt to initiate network connections must go through a mediated path. [\[capability...426\_000545 | PDF\]](https://microsoft-my.sharepoint.com/personal/calvinho_microsoft_com/Documents/Documents/Copilot/Created/capability_native_governance_system_autonomous_ai_agents_20260426_000545.pdf?web=1)

*   **Pod Security Standards:** Ensure Pod Security Admission is set to `baseline` or `restricted` (no host mounts, no privilege, etc.) for agent namespaces. The Fluid Web Previewer compliance checklist requires: no identity/tenant/Copilot context inside the container, network off-by-default with explicitly declared/policy-validated access, brokered host access via constrained messaging, and teardown guarantees between sessions. [\[Validate s...compliance | ADO Work Item (FluidFramework)\]](https://dev.azure.com/fluidframework/235294da-091d-4c29-84fc-cdfc3d90890b/_workitems/edit/70516)

*   **IAM Integration:** Modify the agent launch process to use least-privilege credentials. Each sandbox must provide **strong isolation** — designed explicitly for untrusted workloads, assuming code is malicious by default, applying a least-privilege execution model, restricting system calls, and restricting native module loading. [\[Provision...ox runtime | ADO Work Item (FluidFramework)\]](https://dev.azure.com/fluidframework/235294da-091d-4c29-84fc-cdfc3d90890b/_workitems/edit/70512)

*   **Basic Capability Issuance:** Integrate capability issuance for at least one tool per agent:
    *   Example: For file access, issue a capability that allows reading a specific test file path; for an HTTP call, issue a capability allowing GET to a benign test endpoint.
    *   For MVP, capabilities can be manually configured (hardcoded or simple static manifest) while building integration with IAM for dynamic issuance in Phase 2.

*   **Sandbox Enforcement Hooks:** Connect the reference monitor to these capabilities in a basic way:
    *   If the agent presents a valid token, allow the action; otherwise block and log. The enforcement can be coarse initially (one token unlocks a category of external calls).
    *   Phase 1's focus is containment and proving out the infrastructure; fine-grained tokens will come in Phase 2.

### Workstream C – Observability & Compliance

*   **Unified Logging:** Implement logging for all agent actions:
    *   Instrument reference monitor to log each request's agent ID, action, resource, token ID (if present), decision (allow/deny).
    *   Enable K8s audit logging for security events. Ensure container runtime logs (seccomp/AppArmor denials) are captured.
*   **SIEM Integration:** Create dashboards/alerts for key events:
    *   Denied action count per agent (spike could indicate attempted break-out).
    *   Unexpected process terminations or OOM kills in sandbox pods.
    *   Node health for sandbox node pools.
*   **Kill-Switch Capabilities:** Implement a basic kill-switch:
    *   A ConfigMap that the reference monitor reads to immediately start denying all agent requests (global emergency stop).
    *   Develop a runbook for on-call engineers to trigger kill-switch and verify it halts agent actions within seconds.

### Workstream D – Developer Experience & Testing

*   **Agent Modification:** Adapt 1–2 representative agent applications to run inside the sandbox:
    *   Update agent code to obtain capability tokens from the issuer (or stub them if issuer is not ready).
    *   Reconfigure agents to use the proxy for external calls (e.g., set `HTTP_PROXY` env var to point at reference monitor).
    *   If an in-process policy engine (like AGT) is used, ensure it's compatible: confirm AGT's instrumentation doesn't conflict with sandbox restrictions.
*   **Template & Onboarding Docs:** Develop a Helm chart or docker-compose template for running an agent in the sandbox (covering securityContext, volume mounts, sidecars). Write documentation: "How to run your agent in the sandbox."
*   **Internal Testing:**
    *   Create malicious test harness agents that attempt common attacks (file exfiltration beyond allowed paths, making disallowed network calls, executing `docker` or `kubectl` commands). Run them in the sandbox to verify they are blocked.
    *   Test normal agent behavior to ensure the sandbox doesn't break functionality. Gather feedback from one or two friendly dev teams.

**Milestone Deliverables (end of Phase 1):**

*   **Working MVP Sandbox** deployed in a non-production environment for internal agents:
    *   Agents launch in a locked-down container (no outbound network, no host filesystem, non-root user, minimal syscalls). A new sandbox must be provisioned per generated code execution to prevent taint. [\[Provision...ox runtime | ADO Work Item (FluidFramework)\]](https://dev.azure.com/fluidframework/235294da-091d-4c29-84fc-cdfc3d90890b/_workitems/edit/70512)
    *   Capability reference monitor intercepts initial set of external operations and enforces allow/deny decisions.
    *   Baseline monitoring in place.
*   **Demonstration of containment:** All test cases of unauthorized actions are blocked by the sandbox.
*   **Performance check:** Basic agent tasks run with negligible performance impact (target: overhead per tool invocation under 1ms; memory/CPU usage of sidecar proxies within acceptable range).
*   **Documentation & Templates:** Preliminary developer guide and configuration templates ready for pilot teams.

**Exit Criteria (Phase 1):**

*   ✅ **Containment Verified:** Red-team test scripts (file writes outside allowed paths, disallowed network calls, etc.) fail to execute within the sandbox, with corresponding log events captured.
*   ✅ **Agent Functionality Preserved:** The two sample agent applications complete their normal tasks inside the sandbox without errors.
*   ✅ **Core Components in Place:** Reference monitor functional, baseline security policies enforced cluster-wide, sandbox orchestration automated via CI.
*   ✅ **Stakeholder Go/No-Go:** Security signs off that the MVP meets minimum bar to proceed. Product team representatives concur that performance and dev experience are acceptable for a limited pilot.

***

## Phase 2 (Week 7–10): Pilot – High-Assurance & Cross-Org Capabilities

With the MVP sandbox working for low-risk scenarios, Phase 2 expands the deployment to high-risk contexts and external integrations in a pilot.

### Workstream A – Platform (Enhance Isolation & Scalability)

*   **Enable VM-Based Isolation:** Activate Kata Containers or gVisor for a subset of agent workloads:
    *   On AKS: create a **RuntimeClass** for Kata VM isolation and label one or two high-risk agent deployments to use `runtimeClassName: kata-vm-isolation`. Validate end-to-end: these pods should come up in the Kata-enabled node pool and operate correctly.
    *   **Known AKS Limitations to Plan For:** Kata containers might not reach the IOPS performance limits that traditional containers can reach on Azure Files and high-performance local SSD. **Microsoft Defender for Containers does not support assessing Kata runtime pods**. Kata host-network access is not supported. CPU and memory allocation with Pod Sandboxing has additional considerations compared to `runc`.
    *   On GKE: test the gVisor sandbox on a subset. **Critical constraint:** Container Threat Detection is incompatible with GKE Sandbox and must be disabled on affected node pools. Every cluster must keep at least one non-sandboxed node pool.
    *   On EKS/on-prem: pilot a Firecracker microVM using tools like `firecracker-containerd`.

*   **Warm Pools for Fast Start:** If agent instantiation time is a concern, implement a pool of pre-initialized sandbox pods using the `SandboxWarmPool` controller from `kubernetes-sigs/agent-sandbox`. Pre-warmed pods remain in standby, fully sandboxed but not yet possessing capabilities. When a new agent task arrives, a warm pod is instantly assigned, capability tokens injected, and the agent begins executing with minimal cold-start latency. [\[upscend.com\]](https://www.upscend.com/blogs/how-to-build-innovation-sandbox-in-90-days-playbook)

*   **Scalability & Reliability:** Introduce auto-scaling rules for sandboxed agent pods. Adjust cluster auto-scaler for two node pools (Kata vs. standard pods). Implement a fallback: if Kata-based pods fail or get unschedulable, have a mechanism to fail over to standard pods with alerting.

*   **Cross-Org Networking:** For cross-organizational agent interactions, define specific egress rules to allow connections to partner endpoints with strict domain/IP filters.

### Workstream B – Security & Policy (Hardening)

*   **Fine-Grained Policy & Attenuation:** Implement the Capability Attenuator service for delegating narrower tokens to sub-agents. Enforce no\_new\_privs at the token level in the reference monitor (child tokens must have equal or stricter scopes than parent).

*   **Cross-Org Trust Setup:** Stand up a minimal **trust registry** for cross-org capabilities:
    *   Exchange public keys (DIDs) with one pilot partner organization. Configure the reference monitor to trust the partner's key for incoming tokens.
    *   Align on capability schema for the pilot scenario. Implement parsing and verification in the gateway.

*   **Seccomp/AppArmor Tightening:** Refine the seccomp profile by removing any syscalls found unnecessary during Phase 1. For high-assurance pods, consider a whitelist approach. Add AppArmor rules to explicitly prevent known risky filesystem paths.

*   **Kill-Switch & Quarantine Procedures:** Develop automation to quarantine or restart a sandbox in response to security triggers:
    *   If an agent triggers >N denials in short succession, automatically pause or restart the sandbox and alert security.
    *   Implement "session blacklisting" in the reference monitor: a flag in an allowlist/denylist ConfigMap to have the monitor instantly deny all requests from a suspected rogue agent.

### Workstream C – Observability & Compliance

*   **Monitoring Compatibility Constraints:** Running high-assurance sandboxes (Kata/gVisor) **disables some kernel-level monitoring capabilities.** eBPF-based sensor deployment overhead on standard nodes lands at **1–2.5% CPU and approximately 1% memory per node**, but these sensors face constraints in VM-isolated environments:

| **Monitoring Tool**               | **Standard Container (runc)** | **Kata / gVisor Sandbox**                                |
| --------------------------------- | ----------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------- |
| eBPF-based sensors (Falco, ARMO)  | ✅ Fully functional            | ⚠️ Limited or requires WorkloadAllowlist (GKE Autopilot) |
| Microsoft Defender for Containers | ✅ Supported                   | ❌ Does not support Kata runtime pods                     |
| GKE Container Threat Detection    | ✅ Active                      | ❌ Incompatible with GKE Sandbox on same node pool        |
| Application-level logging         | ✅                             | ✅ (Compensates for reduced kernel-level visibility)      |

*   **Compliance Validation (Critical):** Run formal security tests to validate compliance with internal guidelines:
    *   Validate that each sandbox execution has a fresh environment (no data persists between runs). Per XPIA Risk Review Playbook, the sandbox must not be shared between users/tenants, and each newly generated code run must use a new sandbox to prevent taint. [\[Validate s...compliance | ADO Work Item (FluidFramework)\]](https://dev.azure.com/fluidframework/235294da-091d-4c29-84fc-cdfc3d90890b/_workitems/edit/70516)
    *   Verify no identity tokens or credentials from host leak inside container.
    *   Simulate data exfiltration attempts and verify network egress restrictions block them. The compliance validation scope includes identity/tenant/context isolation, network egress/ingress restrictions, per-run/per-session isolation + teardown, and no "tainted sandbox reuse" across code runs. [\[Validate s...compliance | ADO Work Item (FluidFramework)\]](https://dev.azure.com/fluidframework/235294da-091d-4c29-84fc-cdfc3d90890b/_workitems/edit/70516)
    *   Document results in a "Sandbox Leakage Policy Compliance Report" and have internal security and privacy officers sign off. [\[Validate s...compliance | ADO Work Item (FluidFramework)\]](https://dev.azure.com/fluidframework/235294da-091d-4c29-84fc-cdfc3d90890b/_workitems/edit/70516)

*   **Alert Refinement:** Based on Phase 1 experience, adjust alert thresholds to reduce noise. Implement an "allow list" for benign known issues and focus alerts on truly suspicious patterns.

### Workstream D – Developer Experience & Testing

*   **Pilot Team Onboarding:** Select 1–2 internal product teams to pilot the sandbox in real use. Conduct 1-day workshops with each team to integrate sandbox templates into their deployment pipelines. Use their feedback to improve documentation.

*   **Developer Feedback Loop:** Capture feedback on ease of use, performance impact, and unexpected functional issues. Address critical feedback in-phase.

*   **Tooling for High-Assurance Mode:** Provide a simple toggle for teams (e.g., `sandBoxMode: standard|vm`) that the deployment pipeline interprets to set the correct runtime class. Switching modes should not require code changes.

*   **Cross-Org Scenario Testing:** Coordinate end-to-end testing of an agent from Org A invoking an action in Org B through the capability exchange. Document any issues (e.g., clock skew causing token validation failures).

**Milestone Deliverables (end of Phase 2):**

*   **High-Assurance Sandbox Pilot:** At least one high-risk agent running under VM-level isolation in a test or staging environment on AKS Kata-containers node pool and performing normally.
*   **Cross-Org Capability POC:** A demonstrative transaction using capability tokens between the organization and a partner (or simulated partner) executed successfully in a controlled test.
*   **Policy Hardening Implemented:** No default filesystem, network, or device access for sandboxed agents. Verified through compliance tests.
*   **Monitoring & Alerts Operational:** End-to-end observability confirmed. A test violation triggers seccomp/AppArmor, denial is logged, and alert is sent to on-call. Kill-switch drill conducted.
*   **Developer Feedback Incorporated:** Pilot teams report the sandbox is usable. Documentation updated based on their feedback.

**Exit Criteria (Phase 2):**

*   ✅ *High-Assurance Ready:* The platform can run agents with VM-level isolation, and at least one representative workload functions under that mode with acceptable overhead (e.g., <10% throughput degradation).
*   ✅ *Cross-Org Capability in Beta:* Demonstrated exchange of capabilities with a partner or simulated partner, with a clear plan to productionize.
*   ✅ *Security Sign-off for Production:* Security team satisfied that the sandbox meets policy for hosting sensitive workloads. Compliance validation report reviewed and approved.
*   ✅ *Documentation & Templates Polished:* All pilot participants successfully onboarded using provided templates/docs. A "Sandbox Onboarding Guide" is published for broader teams.

***

## Phase 3 (Week 11–13): Production Rollout – Harden & Scale

Phase 3 transitions the sandbox from pilot to default infrastructure for agent executions.

### Workstream A – Platform (Production Deployment & Scaling)

*   **Rollout Strategy (Graded):**
    *   **Week 11:** Enable sandbox by default for all *new* internal agent deployments in staging. Existing non-sandboxed production agents remain unchanged.
    *   **Week 12:** If no major issues in staging, enable sandbox by default for new production deployments (one product/team at a time). Keep a feature flag for quick bypass if emergency issues arise.
    *   **Week 13:** Migrate remaining long-running agent processes into sandbox. For legacy agents that cannot be easily containerized (e.g., GPU access), handle separately with documented exceptions and timeline to resolve.

*   **Scalability Considerations:** Ensure cluster auto-scaling is tuned for sandbox overhead. Kata pods may use more CPU/memory per agent due to VM overhead; update cluster capacity planning to account for \~15% headroom per node. For on-prem or VM-based sandboxes, verify that VM pools can be created on-demand and destroyed quickly.

*   **Reliability Tools:** Integrate sandbox management into SRE runbooks:
    *   Health checks for the sandbox reference monitor (simulate benign requests in intervals).
    *   Automated restarts for stuck sandboxes (Kubernetes `livenessProbe` for agent and sidecar).
    *   The `Sandbox` CRD supports a `ShutdownPolicy` of either `Delete` or `Retain`, providing flexibility for forensic preservation or immediate cleanup. [\[upscend.com\]](https://www.upscend.com/blogs/how-to-build-innovation-sandbox-in-90-days-playbook)

### Workstream B – Security & Policy (Enterprise Adoption & Governance)

*   **Organization-Wide Policy Enforcement:** Use Policy-as-Code (OPA/Gatekeeper or native Pod Security Admission) to prevent deploying agent pods not meeting sandbox criteria (non-root, correct labels/taints, sidecar present). Implement continuous compliance scans.

*   **Full Attenuation & Delegation Support:** All sub-agent spawns go through the capability attenuation service, inheriting only narrowed privileges from parent. The reference monitor enforces that a child's token carries a reference to a parent token and is no broader.

*   **Full Capability Issuer Integration:** Connect the Capability Issuer to enterprise IAM for dynamic policy:
    *   When an agent for a specific domain launches, it automatically gets a token allowing only the actions appropriate to the user's permissions.
    *   Enforce short TTL on tokens (e.g., 15 minutes). Implement auto-renewal.
    *   Incorporate human approval flows for highest-risk capabilities.

*   **Cross-Org Governance Agreements:** If cross-org use is part of near-term goals, work with Legal/Security to formalize trust exchange agreements (covering liability, revocation processes, audit log sharing in case of incident).

### Workstream C – Observability & Compliance (Operationalization)

*   **SIEM Tuning & Response Automation:** Optimize alert logic to minimize false positives after 2–3 weeks of pilot and early production data. Implement automated incident response runbooks (e.g., if multiple agents exhibit the same suspicious behavior, automatically halt all of them and escalate).

*   **Regular Compliance Audits:** Schedule periodic reviews where security re-runs test batteries to ensure no regressions. Integrate results into compliance reporting.

*   **Performance & Cost Review:** Compare before/after metrics for agent throughput and latency. Evaluate costs of additional isolation. Develop guidelines for isolation mode selection (e.g., "Use Kata for any agent handling customer PII or executing third-party plugins; use runc with seccomp for others").

*   **Production Monitoring Handoff:** Ensure the production monitoring team is trained on new alerts and dashboards. Document runbooks in the Ops knowledge base.

### Workstream D – Developer Experience (Organization-Wide Adoption)

*   **Self-Service Enablement:** Product teams onboard to the sandbox with minimal involvement:
    *   **"Sandbox Integration Package"**: Helm charts or Terraform module, plus example config for common patterns.
    *   Internal training sessions or recorded videos.
    *   Office hours for the first 2–3 weeks to support teams as they integrate.

*   **Developer Tooling Enhancements:**
    *   CLI for capability manifest generation and local testing.
    *   "Dry-run mode" for capabilities: allow an agent to run with full access in staging but log all operations that *would have been denied* under sandbox.

*   **Feedback and Continuous Improvement:** Create a feedback loop for developers. If the process to get a new capability is too slow, refine it.

**Milestone Deliverables (end of Phase 3 / Day 90):**

*   **Broad Deployment:** All targeted agent workloads running in the sandbox by default (enterprise-risk mode containers), with high-assurance mode applied to the most sensitive cases.
*   **Organizational Adoption:** Product teams largely self-sufficient in deploying and operating sandboxed agents.
*   **Performance & Cost Sign-off:** Average agent response time with sandboxing within 5% of previous baseline under production load.
*   **Incident Readiness:** At least one chaos test or live fire drill conducted in production — a simulated compromised agent successfully detected and contained.
*   **Risk Register Updated & Closed:** All major risks addressed or have clear mitigation in place.

**Exit Criteria (Phase 3 & Project Completion):**

*   ✅ *Production Rollout Complete:* The sandbox is the default execution environment for all new agent deployments. Legacy non-sandboxed execution paths deprecated.
*   ✅ *Policy Enforcement Effective:* No agent in production has unrestricted access to network or filesystem. Verified by scanning running processes and network connections.
*   ✅ *Key Metrics Achieved:* 0 security incidents due to agent misbehavior in the first month; 100% of tested attacks blocked in red-team exercises; <1ms added latency per tool call on average.
*   ✅ *Documentation and Handoff:* Detailed runbooks, design docs, and developer guides published. Central platform team transitions sandbox into BAU operations.

***

## Ownership Model: Central Platform vs. Self-Service

| **Responsibility**         | **Central Platform Team**                                                            | **Product Teams (Self-Service)**                                                      |
| -------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| **Sandbox infrastructure** | Owns and operates K8s node pools, VM pools, networking, reference monitor deployment | Consumes via templates; does not manage infrastructure                                |
| **Security policies**      | Defines seccomp profiles, AppArmor rules, NetworkPolicies, admission controllers     | Complies with policies; requests exceptions via defined process                       |
| **Capability issuance**    | Operates the Capability Issuer service; defines capability schemas                   | Specifies required capabilities for their agents via manifest; submits for review     |
| **Monitoring & SIEM**      | Operates logging pipeline, alert rules, dashboards                                   | Consumes dashboards; responds to agent-specific alerts                                |
| **Kill-switch**            | Owns global and session kill-switch mechanisms; defines escalation procedures        | Follows runbook to request agent termination; cannot bypass sandbox                   |
| **Templates & tooling**    | Creates and maintains Helm charts, CLI tools, documentation                          | Uses provided templates; contributes feedback and improvement requests                |
| **Agent code changes**     | Provides SDK/libraries for token management and proxy integration                    | Integrates SDK into their agent code; tests locally using provided dry-run tools      |
| **Compliance sign-off**    | Approves sandbox configurations for production use                                   | Attests that their agent's ACM (capability manifest) accurately reflects requirements |

***

## Upgrade Path: Enterprise-Risk → High-Assurance Sandboxing

| **Dimension**           | **Enterprise-Risk (Day 1 Default)**                             | **High-Assurance (Upgrade)**                                                                          |
| ----------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| **Isolation boundary**  | Standard container (runc) with seccomp/AppArmor                 | Lightweight VM (Kata Containers, Firecracker) with separate kernel per agent                          |
| **Syscall permissions** | Docker/K8s default seccomp (moderately restrictive)             | Nearly all syscalls blocked except essential; deny-by-default whitelist                               |
| **Networking**          | Egress limited to approved domains/IPs via NetworkPolicy        | No direct external egress; all traffic through controlled proxy                                       |
| **Filesystem**          | Read-only root; necessary paths only; non-root user             | Read-only root; ephemeral overlay; no host mounts; only preapproved data                              |
| **Monitoring**          | Full host monitoring via eBPF, Defender, cloud threat detection | Reduced kernel visibility (Defender and CTD incompatible); compensate with application/proxy logging  |
| **Performance**         | Near-native container performance                               | Higher overhead: microVM startup, potential IOPS limitations                                          |
| **Upgrade trigger**     | Default for most workloads                                      | Apply when agent handles customer PII, executes third-party code, or runs in multi-tenant environment |

The upgrade is a **configuration change, not a code change**: switch `runtimeClassName` from default to `kata-vm-isolation` in the Pod spec, or set the sandbox mode flag in the deployment template. The `kubernetes-sigs/agent-sandbox` project supports both isolated runtimes (gVisor and Kata Containers), making the switch transparent to agent code. [\[upscend.com\]](https://www.upscend.com/blogs/how-to-build-innovation-sandbox-in-90-days-playbook)

***

## Risk Register and Mitigations

| **Risk**                                                 | **Phase** | **Impact**                                                                                                               | **Mitigation**                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                          |
| -------------------------------------------------------- | --------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| **Developer pushback / onboarding friction**             | 1–3       | Teams avoid or delay adopting sandbox due to perceived complexity or performance cost                                    | Early communication; involve dev reps in Phase 0 design. Provide templates & CLI tools. Phase 2 pilot to gather feedback. Tie compliance to this feature (no alternative path for production).                                                                                                          |
| **Performance degradation (esp. with Kata/Firecracker)** | 2–3       | Agent response times slow, possibly affecting user experience                                                            | Benchmark in Phase 2; optimize (e.g., enable vCPU pinning for Kata pods). Use warm pools [\[upscend.com\]](https://www.upscend.com/blogs/how-to-build-innovation-sandbox-in-90-days-playbook) to amortize startup costs. For latency-critical paths, consider temporary exception with plan to resolve. |
| **Monitoring blind spots**                               | 2–3       | Reduced visibility (Defender does not support Kata pods; CTD incompatible with GKE Sandbox) leading to undetected issues | Deploy sidecar-based monitoring. Emphasize application-level logs. Include fallback to standard containers for troubleshooting. The runtime behavioral layer stays constant across all architectural decisions.                                                                                         |
| **Compatibility issues with high-assurance tech**        | 2         | Some agent dependencies might not work under gVisor or with certain syscalls blocked                                     | Pilot high-assurance mode on a subset first. If certain features break, those agents remain on standard mode with other mitigations. Provide clear guidance on unsupported patterns.                                                                                                                    |
| **Tight timeline for cross-org integration**             | 2         | Might be ambitious to involve an external partner within 90 days                                                         | If external pilot not feasible, simulate partner internally and run end-to-end tests. True production use with a real partner follows once internal deployment is stable.                                                                                                                               |
| **Unforeseen agent failures in production**              | 3         | A corner-case might cause agent to break under sandbox                                                                   | Staged rollout and feature flag for quick revert. Comprehensive testing in Phase 2. Allow an "escape hatch" (env variable to run unsandboxed for emergency, only accessible to superusers) but require incident ticket for any use.                                                                     |
| **Cultural change and maintenance**                      | 3         | Teams find ways to bypass or neglect updates                                                                             | Executive support: tie sandbox usage to compliance mandate. Implement continuous checks (in CI pipelines or cluster admission) to prevent unsandboxed deployments.                                                                                                                                      |

***

## GKE-Specific Primitive Boundaries

For teams running on GKE, each native primitive has a specific coverage boundary relevant to agent sandboxing:

| **Primitive**                                 | **What It Contributes**                                 | **Where It Stops**                                                                           |
| --------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Cloud Audit Logs                              | API-level audit trail: who called which API, when       | Cannot see what the agent did inside the pod after the API returned                          |
| Workload Identity Federation (KSA principals) | Per-pod IAM identity via short-lived OAuth2 tokens      | Grants the permissions ceiling, not the actual permissions needed                            |
| Container Threat Detection                    | Runtime eBPF detection for known-bad container patterns | Incompatible with GKE Sandbox; ships static rules, no behavioral baselines                   |
| Model Armor                                   | Content-plane screening for prompts and responses       | Operates on the language plane, not on the agent's subsequent behavior                       |
| Agent Sandbox CRD + gVisor                    | Kernel-level isolation for LLM-generated code execution | Controls where the agent runs, not what it does once running. Disables CTD on affected pools |
| Binary Authorization                          | Admission-time image attestation                        | Does not observe runtime behavior; attested image can still host a compromised agent         |

***

## Non-Kubernetes Implementation Track

In environments outside Kubernetes, the same principles apply through OS and virtualization tools:

| **Approach**                              | **Isolation Level**                 | **Overhead**                          | **Best For**                              |
| ----------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------- | ------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------- |
| **Firecracker microVM**                   | Separate kernel, hypervisor-level   | \~125 MB memory, tens of ms startup   | High-assurance; equivalent to Kata in K8s |
| **Docker/Podman with gVisor**             | User-space kernel interception      | 5–10% CPU for syscall-heavy workloads | Moderate assurance; good compatibility    |
| **Docker/Podman with seccomp + AppArmor** | Namespace isolation, kernel sharing | Near-native                           | Enterprise-risk; simplest deployment      |
| **systemd-nspawn / LXC**                  | Namespace isolation                 | Near-native                           | Single-host, process-level isolation      |
| **QEMU/KVM full VM**                      | Complete hardware virtualization    | Higher memory and startup             | Maximum isolation; legacy compatibility   |

**Container engine hardening (non-K8s):** Run with `--security-opt=no-new-privileges`, `--cap-drop=ALL`, custom seccomp profile, `--user` flag for non-root execution, `--read-only` for immutable root filesystem, and `--network=none` for fully offline agents.

**Network isolation on host:** Create a separate Linux network namespace for the agent process or container. Use iptables/nftables on the host to allow/block traffic from the agent's namespace. Run an HTTP proxy on the host as the sole egress path, performing capability checks.

**Process supervision:** Use systemd transient services with sandboxing directives (`PrivateTmp=yes`, `NoNewPrivileges=yes`, `PrivateUsers=yes`, `PrivateNetwork=yes`, `ProtectSystem=strict`). Incorporate health checks and automatic cleanup on crash. Use `pids.max` cgroup to limit processes/threads.

Non-K8s tasks are distributed across phases:

*   **Phase 1:** Implement Docker/Podman-based sandbox with restrictive seccomp profile and reference monitor proxy; validate with the same test harness used for K8s.
*   **Phase 2:** Add Firecracker microVM option for high-assurance non-K8s workloads; validate with compliance tests.
*   **Phase 3:** Provide self-service templates (docker-compose files, systemd unit files, Firecracker launch scripts) for product teams operating outside Kubernetes. The internal "Build configurable sandbox for k8s Platform" task confirms the need for a configuration system supporting multiple deployment targets, generating both docker-compose files for VMSS agentic sandbox environments and Kubernetes YAML files for k8s deployment of agentic sandbox workloads. [\[Build conf...s Platform | ADO Work Items (O365 Exchange)\]](https://dev.azure.com/O365Exchange/959adb23-f323-4d52-8203-ff34e5cbeefa/_workitems/edit/6164639)

***

## Summary Sprint Schedule

| **Week** | **Phase**             | **Platform**                                                             | **Security**                                                   | **Observability**                                      | **DevEx**                                                    |
| -------- | --------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------ | -------------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------ | ------------------------------------------------------------ |
| 0–2      | Design                | Provision dev clusters; finalize architecture                            | Threat model; define seccomp/network profiles                  | Define audit schema; plan SIEM integration             | Develop initial templates; write test plan                   |
| 3–4      | MVP (Sprint 1)        | Deploy sandbox pods, reference monitor stub                              | Enforce deny-egress NetworkPolicy; basic Pod Security          | Stand up logging pipeline; implement basic dashboards  | Modify sample agents; create Helm chart                      |
| 5–6      | MVP (Sprint 2)        | Integrate reference monitor with stub capabilities; validate containment | Tighten seccomp; integrate basic capability checks             | Implement global kill-switch; refine alerts            | Run red-team tests; document results; draft onboarding guide |
| 7–8      | Pilot (Sprint 3)      | Enable Kata/gVisor for subset; set up warm pools                         | Fine-grained attenuation; cross-org trust setup                | Compliance validation tests; SIEM rule expansion       | Onboard pilot teams; gather feedback                         |
| 9–10     | Pilot (Sprint 4)      | Scalability testing; cross-org networking                                | Seccomp/AppArmor tightening; quarantine automation             | Full compliance report; alert threshold tuning         | Cross-org scenario testing; finalize pilot playbook          |
| 11–12    | Production (Sprint 5) | Graded production rollout; auto-scaling tuning                           | Organization-wide policy enforcement via admission controllers | Production monitoring handoff; cost/performance review | Self-service package release; training sessions              |
| 13       | Production (Sprint 6) | Complete migration; legacy cleanup                                       | Full Issuer integration; human approval for high-risk caps     | Periodic compliance audit setup; final sign-off        | Feedback loop established; retrospective                     |

***

By **Day 30**, a basic containment is in place: agents have no ambient network or filesystem access, and all external actions are mediated by a tool gateway (logging any violations). By **Day 60**, the system is hardened with VM-level isolation for high-risk tasks, a successful pilot with real workloads, an expanded policy set including cryptographic delegation and revocation, and validated compliance against internal guidelines requiring per-execution isolation and no tainted sandbox reuse. By **Day 90**, the sandbox is the default for agent execution across the organization, with product teams largely self-sufficient in using it. [\[Validate s...compliance | ADO Work Item (FluidFramework)\]](https://dev.azure.com/fluidframework/235294da-091d-4c29-84fc-cdfc3d90890b/_workitems/edit/70516)

This phased approach delivers immediate value (closing critical holes within weeks) while incrementally layering on advanced features as confidence grows. The dual-tiered sandboxing capability — **enterprise-risk** (lightweight container isolation with comprehensive monitoring) and **high-assurance** (strong VM-based isolation for untrusted or highly sensitive agent tasks) — ensures organizations can match isolation strength to workload risk without imposing unnecessary overhead on lower-risk scenarios. The **central platform team** provides and evolves the sandbox infrastructure and guardrails, while **product teams self-service** their agent deployments within those guardrails, ensuring consistency and security across the enterprise.
